speaker: interviewer
text: This is reel number 6 in my interview with Robert Mendler. You were telling us about your regular workday in Auschwitz-Birkenau. Can you continue to describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. After we started to go to work-- you know, we were marched to work. And, like I say, orchestra play at the gates. We were, we were, we marched for about a mile, two miles, almost, to a factory. I worked for IG Farben company. We built the buildings of-- you know, in the factory. It was hard work. Carried the concrete on my back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Summertime wasn't so bad. In our camp, in the factory-- because we were with the civilians, but we never had-- we weren't allowed to have contact with each other. There were always, there was always a, I should say, prisoner-of-war camp, an English prisoner-of-war camp. They used to go to work always dressed up. Used to sing, used to whistle. You know, always dressed up. Everybody helpful to us, too. They used to get the care packages from England. [INAUDIBLE] we used to work with each other, sometimes took a piece, a bite of chocolate he'd throw away. Same thing with the cigarettes. Lit up a cigarette, took a couple puff, throw it. So we pick it up. Things like this help us in a way, you know. They weren't allowed to have a contact with us at all, as far as that. Because we weren't allowed to have with them, either. Every single day, almost, in the noontime, on the clock-- you could time the clock-- on 1 o'clock, the plane used to come down. Allied plane used to bomb the factory, in daytime. At night, again, also they used to come. The Russian plane used to bomb the factories. And we'd [INAUDIBLE] every day. We had a special place when the alarm came out. Always I remember, the first plane came up. First used to go and make a circle around the sky, weather permit. Made a circle in the sky. A few minutes later, maybe half an hour later, the planes start to come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that they made the circle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They used to mark it, whatever it was, probably-- a marker. They used to come down. They used to bomb the factory. They used to-- unbelievable. We had a certain place. The people, civilian people, they went to shelters. The German, also. But we had a certain place in the factory, on the ground where we lay down. We lay-- and, of course, they can see us, because with the striped uniform-- so never-- they never dropped the bomb where we were laying. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One time, I remember, a fellow-- my friend-- he was-- we were laying like this, on the ground, during the bombing session. And then, later on, the siren start to blow it's all over. I would try to-- I said to him, Shloime, get up. Let's go back-- go back to work. No sound. He didn't say boo. Then I look at him. He was dead. He was hit with a shrapnel from the bomb, right-- and I turned him around-- right in the stomach. Could see all the inside came right out of him. My, oh, my. He used to slept right next to me in the bed. We were very good friends. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He never make it. He didn't know what hit him, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we should go back to work. Lunchtime, they used to bring us, in a thermal bottle, soup from the camp. So that's what you really needed, your shisl and your spoon. Sometimes the soup wasn't so bad. Sometimes you used to find a tapioca, which was OK. It was pretty thick. But sometimes you'll find nail, piece of shoe leather, piece of wood, all kind of crap in the soup. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We worked like this till 7 o'clock evening. Then we march back to camp. At the gates, again, the same routine. Count us out, then music. The orchestra play. The commander would stand down there and count us out every day, and the kapo to report it.

speaker: interviewer
text: If somebody was missing, it was too bad. Because nobody escape. Where you going to go? No place to go. You, you've got the uniform. Your heads were shaved.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, you were, you were recognized right away. You've got the number here. You were recognized. There was no escape. But some people, they did probably escape, or something, but they were captured. Always, coming back for work, we sort of-- back to our power plants, we had to stay for a while. They used to always-- the guillotine, they used to hang the people. Every-- almost every week-- every week, used to hang people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put a sign on him. He tried to escape. He tried to steal something, you know? They put a sign on previous to hang him. We had to observe. Then we had to march around them after they were hanged.

speaker: interviewee
text: We watch them how they hang. We had to observe the hanging. Then back to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were people hung?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I say, they put a sign on him. He, he stole something, or he tried to escape and was captured. They have all kind excuses. They were hanging people for no reason. I guess so. They must have reason. I don't know. Always, every week. Not only Jewish people. There were Pollacks, too. And a man one time-- a fellow was saying-- he would start to-- he was singing Polish, Polish national anthem. And they will hang him. And, all of a sudden, bang-- hang him right there. They always hang three at a time. Never forget-- always three at a time. Then we would go back to the barracks. Got another slice of bread, coffee, and a soup. This was our diet, every day. Once in a while, we had day off. We had to wash our uniforms. Always used to be before the winter, like in November. It was cold as heck. You know, you can't hang the, you can't hang the clothes outside, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wash it out. They gave you soap, and you wash the uniform. So we use to sometime-- so we would run out naked, like that. A little bit sunny, it was OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, later on, you put it-- took in the barrack. You slept on it, to dry up. Next day, to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once in a while we got a day off. But we also had in the camp entertainment. I went to their theatres. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had the soccer matches, boxing matches in Birkenau. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in the Appellplatz, they played the soccer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We also had a-- they used to, they used to have-- one barrack was all fenced in with, with the fence, with the barbed wires. They used to bring the women from Auschwitz for the pleasure for the kapos and for the SS troops. Every day they used to bring different women. Because how much women can take, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I don't think you'd see, see many kapos go down there. But most of the SS troopers used to go. It was right in the middle-- in the, in the, in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the religious observance like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Religious observance in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Religious observance in Auschwitz. When you go and you come back from work, you went to the latrine. You were already free in the barrack, in the camp. So you go to the latrine. People used to gather in-- I used to go visit my friends' barracks, too. Every barrack had his leader, called the Blockalteste. My Blockalteste was a German fellow. He was, he was in camp for murder. He was a murderer. He had a, he had a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Black triangle.

speaker: interviewee
text: --purple triangle. He was a, he was a murderer. But he was so strict. The beds-- we slept on the straw sack. The bed has to-- he had a big, like, a big yardstick-- long stick. He put on one end of the bed, maybe for about 10 different beds. It had to be aways even. If your bed wasn't made right, your ration was cut out, and he'd beat the heck out of you. He was, he was, he was a, he was a murderer. He was a sadist. You talk about the religion things. So we used get in the washroom. And I see many-- you know, the Hungarian Jews, some Polish Jews, they used to go in a corner and they used to pray. They used to daven. They used to daven. Yeah. Majority didn't pay attention. Remember one thing, I didn't know what day it was. I didn't know what month is was, what week. I didn't know what year it was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I only knew it was day and night-- that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people in the camp that were keeping track of these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose. Religious people, maybe they did. I didn't. You know, I wasn't believer. I lost my faith. I didn't care what happened. I didn't give a damn.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was keeping you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: My will, strong will to live. I wanted to live. Because I wanted-- I always thought to myself, maybe I see my mom and my sister. I didn't know what happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the day I came to Birkenau-- I should have mentioned it before to you. The day I came to Birkenau was a terrible day for me. Because my brother came in from different camps. He was in Tarnow. From Nowy Sacz, they'd sent him to Tarnow. He came to the Birkenau, also, the same day. There's no time. They take the whole transport. They put it right away in the gas chamber. I didn't have a chance to see him. But some guys, they survived from this transport. You know, you always ask. So I always ask. New transports arrive, so I always-- where do you come from, did you see my brother, or something like that, you know. So I find out from one fellow. He came from Tarnow, went off straight. He was older than me. He was stronger than me, healthier than me. I guess he wasn't lucky as I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother had been living with your grandfather. Did you find out-- in Nowy Sacz, did you find out what happened to your grandfather, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather died before the war, yet. I call my grandfather, grandfather home, really, but my father brothers-- two brothers and four sister live there with the family in Nowy Sacz. So my brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to my brother, really, you know, during the war, he lived with them in Nowy Sacz, because on the black market he used to buy-- he was already 16 years old. He used to buy sugar. He used to make candy on the side, you know, and help us out. It was a bigger city, bigger Jewish population. They were more free than we were, you know. So he used to live down there, and he used to help us out. But I hadn't seen him since '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- how long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Auschwitz in 1945. The January 1945, January 18, I left Birkenau. We left Auschwitz. We were burning the barracks. I was one of the last transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we did-- we came from work. We worked to the last minute. The order came out we have to evacuate the camp. We're allowed to take our blankets with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They give us extra ration. They give-- instead of once slice of bread, we got two slices of bread and a soup. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we started to--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was one of the last transport. But what happened-- Dr. Mengele had his hospital in Buna, down there. That's where he was performing his experiment-- different things. Was a big hospital down there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were a lot of kids. He left kids scattered behind. So there were some people that just ran away. Because we knew there were Russian coming in. But in the meantime, the camp was liberated nine days later. We thought for sure they'd be liberated the next day. Because you could see the sky was all red. They passed Krakow. From Krakow to Auschwitz is only around 40 kilometers. And we thought they're for sure going to liberate. So some people remained in the, in the Buna, in, in the, in the camp. And they left some children, too. But we marched--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that they left them behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was the one-- the last transport that left Auschwitz. We were burning the barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I seen, you know, that certain barracks we didn't, we didn't bother burn because we left the kitchen down there. We left the thing-- And so we left Auschwitz. We marched. You know, snow was high. It was cold. It was January. And down there, you get 30-below-zero weather. Snow is so high. You walked in wooden shoes. Snow builds up. You know, you can hardly walk. But I used to work outside, on the baukommando, like I said. So I used to wrap myself with the paper from the cement bags-- we used to empty cement bags-- around my body and my feet. Meantime, my feet, you know, they froze up to the paper. But we marched. So we marched the whole night, the whole day, and the whole night. Early in the morning, we reach the destination, Gliwice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately how many people were on the death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, about 10,000. Over 10,000. We marched through the city, Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Things was so bad. People were dying, you know. And the SS trooper, they used to walk with us, too. But they used to get food. They used to feed them. But they didn't feed us, because we got ration for-- extra ration. People were dying. I had become, myself, a muselmann. A muselmann-- it's-- a person become muselmann got diarrhea. And this is the worst thing you can do. You got temperature. Your head start to swell up-- your body, your face. And I was-- I just want to die. I just-- and my friends from my hometown, we used to stick together. Romek, you must go. You must go. They're carrying me. They're carrying me. Finally we reach Gliwice. I was little bit better. But I was getting to be a muselmann. I survived, but they didn't, unfortunately. They were stronger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we come to Gliwice. They used to kill people-- these people tried to escape on the way to Gliwice, you know, because they were hoping they will be, they'd be liberated the next day by the Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some escaped. Because I came across a fellow in Washington, DC. He escaped, and he survived. He was with me on the march. We left Auschwitz together. He recognized me. And so he told me about it. Obviously, I'll tell you later on. And so we came to the camp there. There was a camp, a small camp, in Gliwice. We stay there for a few hours. Then we have to go march to the railroad station. There were open boxcars, like you haul coal here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They load us up in the boxcars, the whole camp  from Auschwitz-- from Auschwitz, from Birkenau, and from Buna. All the people came to Gliwice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some maybe went to different places. They load them up on transports, ship them out right away. But we loaded up on the open boxcar. There were 150, maybe, of us. We were packed like sardines-- tight, which wasn't so bad, because you were-- at least you were warmer-- stick to each other. Then the trains start to move. It was so cold. It was snowing. They're going to take us to Oranienburg, which only should take us five hours to travel from Gliwice to Oranienburg or Sachsenhausen, one of the two camps. We travel for 10 days from Auschwitz to Prague, Czechoslovakia, from Prague to Vienna, Austria, and then from Vienna to Berlin. We had a big triangle, like.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we went through the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to get out of boxcars?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but you could see it. We know where we were. When we arrived to the station, when the train stopped in the station, you know. And when we would travel through-- from-- through Czechoslovakia, the people that were standing on the bridges, they were throwing breads to the train. Whoever caught piece were lucky. But the meantime, it was so cold. It was so terrible. People died. Start to-- they, they were-- we were hungry. Nothing to eat. We travelled without any food, without any water, for 10 days, nothing to eat. I swear to God, we ate the dead bodies. People should die. We used to bite them, as long as, as long as they were a little bit warmer. Then they froze up. You couldn't chew on them. We used to cover up our self when we're laying with the dead bodies. Because it was so-- wind was blowing. The snow was coming down. It was a terrible winter, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally we reach to, in Berlin. We were staying in station Berlin for a while, the rail station. Then they throw the breads to, to the, to the trains. Whoever caught something-- By then, there wasn't too many of us left alive on the train. In our, in our, in my boxcar, there wasn't too many left of us. Used to throw the bodies out when the train was moving, too, because they were so stiff. And what good is it to them? You just take off the clothes and wrap yourself with their clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally we reach Oranienburg. We got out the train. I spent a few days in Oranienburg in an ammunition factory, I believe it was. Or was it-- or maybe it was the airplane factory. One of them two. Because from Oranienburg, took us to Sachsenhausen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I spent a few days down there. And take us again to Sachsenhausen. Again, open, open train, we travelled like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I work in the Sachsenhausen, with the plane factory [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for a few weeks, couple weeks. And, later on, the Russian were coming, again. They took us to Flossenburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: We travelled to Flossenburg in the closed cars. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we arrived in Sachsenhausen, before I went to Flossenburg yet, friend of mine-- my very good friend of mine-- he just couldn't take it anymore. I carry him. I was already a little bit stronger. I don't know how, but I was a little bit stronger. I carried-- he died in my hands. He said to me, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Which means, 'Give my regards to my mother, if you see her.' He died in my hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dolek Nas. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He died in my arms. I left him on the train. What am I going to do with him? Stayed down there in, in Sachsenhausen for a little while. Then they send us to Flossenburg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Flossenburg-- we arrive in the station down there. We marched-- the station was down below, and the camp was up. So we marched from the station to the camp. Flossen was a terrible camp, just like Birkenau. They have crematoriums. They put us in a quarantine. We walked like zombies, without any clothes. Put a numbers here on our foreheads, with the orange marker. I never forget. We was in a quarantine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I arrive in, in Flossenburg in February, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '45. Finally, after a few weeks in quarantine, I got a job. Put me in the barracks, way on top. I don't remember the number of the barracks I stay. But the barracks were on top.

speaker: interviewee
text: The kitchen was on the bottom. The bath house was also down. The gas chamber, also. Crematorium, I should say. I got a job. Back to stone quarry. They knew I worked in the stone quarry, so they put me back to stone quarry. It was hard work. I worked so hard. Because the food was terrible. Not much to eat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The, the-- and the, the, the people, and, and the prisoners, themself-- everybody for itself. You know, even in the barrack where you slept, sometimes you find somebody, you get acquainted with each other, you know, become friends. I had one friend with me. We went all through the camps together. He lives in Antwerp, in Belgium. He's alive today. We were together all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I spent time in Flossenburg. It was a terrible camp, especially in quarantine. When the-- before, when we came-- went to take our baths in the bath house. They opened up the windows. It was so cold. The wind was blowing. It was cold. And the hot water, they pour on us. Then the cold water. Then the hot water. Then the cold water.

speaker: interviewee
text: They want you to get pneumonia, or they want to kill us all. They didn't have no time even to kill you, because there were so many people coming in. They were, they were too busy to killing others. But whoever was lucky. People used to die. That was terrible. It was terrible camp, Flossenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the stone quarry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stone quarry was on top, there. Used to go. We used to-- they used to blast the stone. And we used to break the stone-- load them up. And they were shipping out. You know, sometime you used to work from one place to another. You dig out, you throw the stones here, then next day you throw the stone back on this place, just to keep you slave. It was a hard work. I took my wife to Israel. Went to Yad Vashem. I see the picture. I said, before I went-- had a chance to see where it was, I recognized the place where I worked, right there from Flossenburg. I recognized right away the place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was in Flossenburg to April 1. How many people were still with you from Nowy Targ? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: None.

speaker: interviewer
text: None.

speaker: interviewee
text: None. I was the only one, myself. All my friends, they were in different camps. They split us. And most of them, they die. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was sent-- April 1, I went to Pocking-- small camp called Pocking, in Bavaria. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came down April 1, 1945, 400 men. We're supposed to build airfield for the Stuka jets, the small jets plane, which they-- which we were, were doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came down to 400 men. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think this was the worst camp I experienced, really. Because we stay in one barracks. There was no water. Sanitation, why, it was terrible. We were all loused up. You know, the louse going through the skin, behind where the hair grows, inside. And they always bite-- suck the blood out of you. You scratch yourself, scratch yourself. You were all loused up. Wherever your hair grow-- everything. There was no water running. There was no toilet to go to. They had a barrel in our barrack. One barrack, we slept. One. There was no bed. We slept on the, on the ground, on the straw. You could see the straw was walking. It was so loused up, you could see the straw was walking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a barrel to go to the bathroom at night. So we went to the barrel. In the morning, they took the barrel-- dump out in a field. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] About two days-- because this was a terrible camp. We worked very hard. One time, I never forget, we were pushing the railroad cars. And I seen a bone. Must have-- some kind of animal bone. Old bone. I picked the bone out, and I stick it behind my shirt. And every day, with a spoon, I scrape a little bit the bone so I could have something to eat, you know. Satisfy my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Craving.

speaker: interviewee
text: --hunger, whatever it was, a little bit, you know. And they used to-- Allied plane used to come. One time, they had a raid. And they had the-- German had a field kitchen in a, in a, on the railroad, in the cars. And they run away. The Allied planes start to come down, start to shoot. Where we were building, they were knocking down.

speaker: interviewer
text: We ran to the, to the boxcar, that kitchen, down there. They had a big kettle with, with scrambled eggs. So I grabbed some. I stuck in my pocket, behind my shirt. Still have a mark here [LAUGHS] for burn, you know? Didn't care. Somebody going to get killed, or what. In the-- So we spent down there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all one month. OK, we'll stop. We'll stop there.
